Statistic.name: ListSort_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Working Memory (List Sorting) | columnHeader: ListSort_Unadj | description: This task assesses working memory and requires the participant to sequence different visually- and orally-presented stimuli (foods and animals) into size order. List Sorting is a measure of working memory, tapping both information processing and storage. It is considered a “fluid ability” measure, with performance tending to peak in early adulthood and then declining across the life span. Higher scores on each of these indicate higher levels of working memory. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox List Sorting Working Memory Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: ListSort_AgeAdj
Statistic.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Working Memory (List Sorting) | columnHeader: ListSort_AgeAdj | description: This task assesses working memory and requires the participant to sequence different visually- and orally-presented stimuli (foods and animals) into size order. List Sorting is a measure of working memory, tapping both information processing and storage. It is considered a “fluid ability” measure, with performance tending to peak in early adulthood and then declining across the life span. Higher scores on each of these indicate higher levels of working memory. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox List Sorting Working Memory Test: Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: CogFluidComp_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Cognition Fluid Composite | columnHeader: CogFluidComp_Unadj | description: The Fluid Cognition Composite score is derived by averaging the normalized scores of each of the Toolbox tests that are fluid ability measures (Flanker, Dimensional Change Card Sort, Picture Sequence Memory, List Sorting and Pattern Comparison), then deriving scale scores based on this new distribution. One can interpret the Fluid Cognition Composite as a more global assessment of individual and group fluid cognition functioning. Higher scores indicate higher levels of functioning. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Cognition Fluid Composite: Unadjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: CogFluidComp_AgeAdj
Statistic.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Cognition Fluid Composite | columnHeader: CogFluidComp_AgeAdj | description: The Fluid Cognition Composite score is derived by averaging the normalized scores of each of the Toolbox tests that are fluid ability measures (Flanker, Dimensional Change Card Sort, Picture Sequence Memory, List Sorting and Pattern Comparison), then deriving scale scores based on this new distribution. One can interpret the Fluid Cognition Composite as a more global assessment of individual and group fluid cognition functioning. Higher scores indicate higher levels of functioning. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Cognition Fluid Composite: Age Adjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: CogEarlyComp_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Cognition Early Childhood Composite | columnHeader: CogEarlyComp_Unadj | description: The Early Childhood Composite score is derived by averaging the normalized scores of each of the four Toolbox cognition measures that comprise the Early Childhood Battery (Picture Vocabulary, Flanker, DCCS and Picture Sequence Memory), then deriving scale scores based on this new distribution. The Early Childhood Composite Score can be interpreted much like the Cognitive Function Composite and it provides a highly reliable overall snapshot of general cognitive functioning. Higher scores indicate higher levels of functioning. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Cognition Early Childhood Composite: Unadjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: AngAffect_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Negative Affect (Sadness, Fear, Anger) | columnHeader: AngAffect_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses anger as an emotion for ages 18-85, using a CAT format. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more feelings of anger (irritability, frustration). Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of angry feelings and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of angry feelings. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Anger-Affect Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Psychological Well-being (Positive Affect, Life Satisfaction, Meaning and Purpose) | columnHeader: MeanPurp_Unadj | description: This self-report measure for adults (ages 18 and above) is a CAT that accesses the extent to which people feel their life matters or makes sense. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores indicate more self-reported meaning and purpose. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of meaning and purpose and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of meaning and purpose. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Meaning and Purpose Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Psychological Well-being (Positive Affect, Life Satisfaction, Meaning and Purpose).MeanPurp_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Psychological Well-being (Positive Affect, Life Satisfaction, Meaning and Purpose)

Statistic.name: PosAffect_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Psychological Well-being (Positive Affect, Life Satisfaction, Meaning and Purpose) | columnHeader: PosAffect_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses both activated (i.e., happiness, joy) as well as unactivated (i.e., serenity, peace) aspects of positive affect in CAT form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more positive affect. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of positive affect and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of positive affect. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Positive Affect Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Psychological Well-being (Positive Affect, Life Satisfaction, Meaning and Purpose).PosAffect_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Psychological Well-being (Positive Affect, Life Satisfaction, Meaning and Purpose)

Statistic.name: Friendship_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: Friendship_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses perceptions of friendship, using an 8-item fixed-length form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of a greater perceived availability of companions with whom to interact or affiliate. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of friendship and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of friendship. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Friendship Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).Friendship_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Statistic.name: Loneliness_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: Loneliness_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses perceptions of loneliness using a 5-item fixed-length form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more loneliness. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of loneliness and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of loneliness. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Loneliness Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).Loneliness_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Statistic.name: PercHostil_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: PercHostil_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses perceptions of hostility in daily social interactions (e.g., how often people argue with me, yell at me, or criticize me) using an 8-item fixed-length form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of greater perceived hostility. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of perceived hostility and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of perceived hostility. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Perceived Hostility Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).PercHostil_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Statistic.name: PercReject_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: PercReject_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses perceptions of rejection in daily social interactions (e.g., how often people don’t listen when I ask for help, or do not pay attention to me) using an 8-item fixed-length form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of greater perceived rejection. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of perceived rejection and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of perceived rejection. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Perceived Rejection Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).PercReject_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Statistic.name: EmotSupp_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: EmotSupp_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses emotional support (refers to the perception that people in one’s social network are available to listen to one’s problems with empathy, caring and understanding) through an 8-item fixed-length form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more emotional support. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of support,and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of support. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Emotional Support Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).EmotSupp_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Statistic.name: InstruSupp_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: InstruSupp_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses instrumental support (refers to the perception that people in one’s social network are available to provide material or functional aid in completing daily tasks, if needed) for ages 18-85, using an 8-item fixed-length form. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. higher scores are indicative of more reported support. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of instrumental supportand scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of instrumental support. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Instrumental Support Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).InstruSupp_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Statistic.name: PercStress_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy) | columnHeader: PercStress_Unadj | description: This is a self-report measure administered to ages 13-85 in CAT form that accesses how unpredictable, uncontrollable and overloadedrespondents find their lives. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more perceived stress. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of perceived stress and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of perceived stress. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Perceived Stress Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy).PercStress_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy)

Statistic.name: SelfEff_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy) | columnHeader: SelfEff_Unadj | description: This is a self-report measure administered to ages 8-85 in CAT form. It assesses respondents' sense of global self-efficacy (a person’s belief in his/her capacity to manage functioning and have control over meaningful events). The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more general self-efficacy. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of general self-efficacy and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of general self-efficacy. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Self-Efficacy Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy).SelfEff_Unadj

Statistic.name: Endurance_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Endurance (2 minute walk test) | columnHeader: Endurance_Unadj | description: This test measures sub-maximal cardiovascular endurance by recording the distance that the participant is able to walk on a 50-foot (out and back) course in 2 minutes.  The participant's raw score is the distance in feet and inches walked in 2 minutes. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant raw score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 2-minute Walk Endurance Test : Unadjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: Endurance_AgeAdj
Statistic.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Endurance (2 minute walk test) | columnHeader: Endurance_AgeAdj | description: This test measures sub-maximal cardiovascular endurance by recording the distance that the participant is able to walk on a 50-foot (out and back) course in 2 minutes.  The participant's raw score is the distance in feet and inches walked in 2 minutes. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant raw score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 2-minute Walk Endurance Test : Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: GaitSpeed_Comp
Statistic.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Locomotion (4-meter walk test) | columnHeader: GaitSpeed_Comp | description: Participants are asked to walk a short distance (4 meters) at their usual pace. Raw scores are recorded as the time in seconds required to walk 4 meters on each of the two trials, with the better trial used for scoring (raw scores are available on request to info@humanconnectome.org). Computed scores are in meters per second. On the 4-Meter Walk Gait Speed Test, higher computed scores are indicative of better gait speed (i.e., fewer seconds to walk four meters). | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 4-Meter Walk Gait Speed Test: Computed Score

Statistic.name: Dexterity_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Dexterity (9-hole Pegboard) | columnHeader: Dexterity_Unadj | description: This test of manual dexterity records the time required for the participant to accurately place and remove 9 plastic pegs into a plastic pegboard. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. Higher scoring is indicative of better dexterity in the dominant hand. For the 9-Hole Pegboard Dexterity Test, the raw score, recorded as time in seconds that it takes the participant to complete the task, is commonly used for interpretation, with faster completion times (less time to complete) representing better manual dexterity. This also allows for raw comparisons between dominant and non-dominant hand performance. Raw scores are available on request to info@humanconnectome.org. However, one can also evaluate performance with the dominant hand by looking at the normative scale scores provided here. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 9-hole Pegboard Dexterity Test : Unadjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: Dexterity_AgeAdj
Statistic.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Dexterity (9-hole Pegboard) | columnHeader: Dexterity_AgeAdj | description: This test of manual dexterity records the time required for the participant to accurately place and remove 9 plastic pegs into a plastic pegboard. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. Higher scoring is indicative of better dexterity in the dominant hand. For the 9-Hole Pegboard Dexterity Test, the raw score, recorded as time in seconds that it takes the participant to complete the task, is commonly used for interpretation, with faster completion times (less time to complete) representing better manual dexterity. This also allows for raw comparisons between dominant and non-dominant hand performance. Raw scores are available on request to info@humanconnectome.org. However, one can also evaluate performance with the dominant hand by looking at the normative scale scores provided here. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 9-hole Pegboard Dexterity Test : Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: Strength_Unadj
Statistic.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Strength (Grip Strength Dynamometry) | columnHeader: Strength_Unadj | description: Grip strength for each hand is measured with the participant seated in a chair with their feet touching the ground. With the elbow bent to 90 degrees and the arm against the trunk, wrist at neutral, participants squeeze the Jamar Plus Digital dynamometer as hard as they can for a count of three. The dynamometer provides a digital reading of force in pounds.  each hand. The Grip Strength Test provides a raw score for each hand (raw scores available on request to info@humanconnectome.org), with the scale scores representing the relative force the participant was able to generate using his/her dominant hand. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant raw score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Grip Strength Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Statistic.name: Strength_AgeAdj
Statistic.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Strength (Grip Strength Dynamometry) | columnHeader: Strength_AgeAdj | description: Grip strength for each hand is measured with the participant seated in a chair with their feet touching the ground. With the elbow bent to 90 degrees and the arm against the trunk, wrist at neutral, participants squeeze the Jamar Plus Digital dynamometer as hard as they can for a count of three. The dynamometer provides a digital reading of force in pounds.  each hand. The Grip Strength Test provides a raw score for each hand (raw scores available on request to info@humanconnectome.org), with the scale scores representing the relative force the participant was able to generate using his/her dominant hand. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant raw score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Grip Strength Test: Age-Adjusted-Adjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: HasGT

Family structure (i.e., Mother_ID and Father_ID) is genetically verified for pairs of siblings (includings twins and half-silbings) for whom HasGT=TRUE | fullDisplayName: HasGT

Delay Condition.name: ZygositySR
Delay Condition.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: ZygositySR | description: Self-reported zygosity. Until the S1200 release this was the \Zygosity\" measure. The small number of subjects who do not have a value for this measure (blank) self reported as twins but did not self report their twin zygosity." | fullDisplayName: ZygositySR

Delay Condition.name: ZygosityGT
Delay Condition.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: ZygosityGT | description: Twin zygosity verified by genotyping. Requires both subjects in a twin pair to have HasGT=TRUE to have a value (if genotyping is not available for either of a twin pair, no values are given for ZygosityGT).  Non-twin subjects also do not have a value for ZygosityGT.  Note that some subjects self-reported as dizygotic twins (ZygositySR='NotMZ') but genotyping established that they were monozygotic twins (thence ZygosityGT='MZ' for those subjects).  ZygosityGT should be given precedence over ZygositySR. | fullDisplayName: ZygosityGT

Delay Condition.name: QC_Issue
Delay Condition.desc: category: QC Issues | assessment: Quality Control Issue Noted | columnHeader: QC_Issue | description: Subjects with notable issues (coded A-E) found in the HCP Quality Control process. For more information, see the QC Issues wiki: A= Focal anatomical anomaly (e.g. calcification, cavernoma, cyst, etc.) found in T1w and/or T2w scans in a restricted brain region; B= Focal segmentation or surface error(s) in outputs from the HCP Structural pipeline; C= Subject data collected during period of known intermittent problems with head coil leading to temporal instability in acquisitions; D= One or more rfMRI scans contained significant coil- or movement-related artifact which manifests prominently in the \"minimally preprocessed\" data. FIX cleaned data has been confirmed to be reasonable for all these scans; E= Manual reclassification of some of the FIX-ICA components from 'signal' to 'noise' was necessary to appropriately remove artifact from the FIX-denoised rfMRI scans. | fullDisplayName: Coded QC Issue

Delay Condition.name: PicSeq_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Episodic Memory (Picture Sequence Memory) | columnHeader: PicSeq_Unadj | description: The Picture Sequence Memory Test is an assessment of episodic memory for ages 3-85 years which involves the acquisition, storage and effortful recall of new information. It is considered a strong 'fluid ability' measure, with performance reaching a peak in early adulthood and declining across the life span. It involves recalling increasingly lengthy series of illustrated objects and activities that are presented in a particular order on the computer screen. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Picture Sequence Memory Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: PicSeq_AgeAdj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Episodic Memory (Picture Sequence Memory) | columnHeader: PicSeq_AgeAdj | description: The Picture Sequence Memory Test is an assessment of episodic memory for ages 3-85 years which involves the acquisition, storage and effortful recall of new information. It is considered a strong 'fluid ability' measure, with performance reaching a peak in early adulthood and declining across the life span. It involves recalling increasingly lengthy series of illustrated objects and activities that are presented in a particular order on the computer screen. Age-Adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Picture Sequence Memory Test: Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: CardSort_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Executive Function/Cognitive Flexibility (Dimensional Change Card Sort) | columnHeader: CardSort_Unadj | description: The Dimensional Change Card Sort Test is a measure of executive function, specifically tapping cognitive flexibility for ages 3-85. It is considered a 'fluid ability' measure, with performance generally increasing through childhood and then declining across the adult age span. Target pictures are presented that vary along two dimensions (e.g., shape and color). Participants are asked to match a series of bivalent test pictures (e.g., yellow balls and blue trucks) to the target pictures, first according to one dimension (e.g., color) and then, after a number of trials, according to the other dimension (e.g., shape). Switch trials are also employed, in which the participant must change the dimension being matched, thus requiring the cognitive flexibility to quickly choose the correct stimulus. Scoring is based on a combination of accuracy and reaction time. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Dimensional Change Card Sort Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: Flanker_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Executive Function/Inhibition (Flanker Task) | columnHeader: Flanker_Unadj | description: The Flanker is a measure of executive function, specifically tapping inhibitory control and attention for ages 3-85. It is considered a fluid ability measure (i.e. measures the capacity for new learning and information processing in novel situations) in which performance reaches a peak in early adulthood, then tends to decline across the life span. The test requires the participant to focus on a given stimulus while inhibiting attention to stimuli (arrows for ages 8-85) flanking it. Sometimes the middle stimulus is pointing in the same direction as the flankers (congruent) and sometimes in the opposite direction (incongruent). Scoring is based on a combination of accuracy and reaction time. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Flanker Inhibitory Control and Attention Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Executive Function/Inhibition (Flanker Task) | columnHeader: Flanker_AgeAdj | description: The Flanker is a measure of executive function, specifically tapping inhibitory control and attention for ages 3-85. Delay Condition: 24

Delay Condition.name: PMAT24_A_CR
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Fluid Intelligence (Penn Progressive Matrices) | columnHeader: PMAT24_A_CR | description: Penn Matrix Test (PMAT): Number of Correct Responses. The PMAT measures fluid intelligence via non-verbal reasoning using an abbreviated version of the Raven's Progressive Matrices Form A developed by Gur and colleagues (Bilker et al. 2012).  Participants are presented with patterns made up of 2x2, 3x3 or 1x5 arrangements of squares, with one of the squares missing.  The participant must pick one of five response choices that best fits the missing square on the pattern.  The task has 24 items and 3 bonus items, arranged in order of increasing difficulty.  However, the task discontinues if the participant makes 5 incorrect responses in a row. | fullDisplayName: Penn Progressive Matrices: Number of Correct Responses (PMAT24_A_CR)

Delay Condition.name: PMAT24_A_SI
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Fluid Intelligence (Penn Progressive Matrices) | columnHeader: PMAT24_A_SI | description: Penn Matrix Test (PMAT): Total Skipped Items (items not presented because maximum errors allowed reached [5 in a row]). The PMAT measures fluid intelligence via non-verbal reasoning using an abbreviated version of the Raven's Progressive Matrices Form A developed by Gur and colleagues (Bilker et al. 2012).  Participants are presented with patterns made up of 2x2, 3x3 or 1x5 arrangements of squares, with one of the squares missing.  The participant must pick one of five response choices that best fits the missing square on the pattern.  The task has 24 items and 3 bonus items, arranged in order of increasing difficulty.  However, the task discontinues if the participant makes 5 incorrect responses in a row. | fullDisplayName: Penn Progressive Matrices: Total Skipped Items (PMAT24_A_SI)

Delay Condition.name: PMAT24_A_RTCR
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Fluid Intelligence (Penn Progressive Matrices) | columnHeader: PMAT24_A_RTCR | description: Penn Matrix Test (PMAT): Median Reaction Time (RT) for Correct Responses. The RT measure can be combined with the Correct Responses measure to gauge PMAT efficiency. The PMAT measures fluid intelligence via non-verbal reasoning using an abbreviated version of the Raven's Progressive Matrices Form A developed by Gur and colleagues (Bilker et al. 2012).  Participants are presented with patterns made up of 2x2, 3x3 or 1x5 arrangements of squares, with one of the squares missing.  The participant must pick one of five response choices that best fits the missing square on the pattern.  The task has 24 items and 3 bonus items, arranged in order of increasing difficulty.  However, the task discontinues if the participant makes 5 incorrect responses in a row. | fullDisplayName: Penn Progressive Matrices: Median Reaction Time for Correct Responses (PMAT24_A_RTCR)

Delay Condition.name: ReadEng_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Language/Reading Decoding (Oral Reading Recognition) | columnHeader: ReadEng_Unadj | description: The Reading Test is a CAT format measure of reading decoding skill and of crystallized abilities, those abilities that are generally more dependent upon past learning experiences and consistent across the life span for ages 7-85. The participant is asked to read and pronounce letters and words as accurately as possible. Higher scores indicate better reading ability. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Oral Reading Recognition Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: ReadEng_AgeAdj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Language/Reading Decoding (Oral Reading Recognition) | columnHeader: ReadEng_AgeAdj | description: The Reading Test is a CAT format measure of reading decoding skill and of crystallized abilities, those abilities that are generally more dependent upon past learning experiences and consistent across the life span for ages 7-85. The participant is asked to read and pronounce letters and words as accurately as possible. Higher scores indicate better reading ability. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Oral Reading Recognition Test: Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: PicVocab_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Language/Vocabulary Comprehension (Picture Vocabulary) | columnHeader: PicVocab_Unadj | description: The Picture Vocabulary Test is a CAT format measure of general vocabulary knowledge for ages 3-85 and is considered to be a strong measure of crystallized abilities (those abilities that are more dependent upon past learning experiences and are consistent across the lifespan). The participant is presented with an audio recording of a word and four photographic images on the computer screen and is asked to select the picture that most closely matches the meaning of the word. Higher scores indicate higher vocabulary ability. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Picture Vocabulary Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: PicVocab_AgeAdj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Language/Vocabulary Comprehension (Picture Vocabulary) | columnHeader: PicVocab_AgeAdj | description: The Picture Vocabulary Test is a CAT format measure of general vocabulary knowledge for ages 3-85 and is considered to be a strong measure of crystallized abilities (those abilities that are more dependent upon past learning experiences and are consistent across the lifespan). The participant is presented with an audio recording of a word and four photographic images on the computer screen and is asked to select the picture that most closely matches the meaning of the word. Higher scores indicate higher vocabulary ability. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Picture Vocabulary Test: Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: ProcSpeed_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Processing Speed (Pattern Completion Processing Speed) | columnHeader: ProcSpeed_Unadj | description: The Pattern Comparison Processing Test is a measure of speed of processing, which is considered a 'fluid ability' because it steadily improves (time to complete task decreases) throughout childhood and adolescence, then begins to decline in adulthood. This test for ages 7-85 asks participants to discern whether two side-by-side pictures are the same or not. The items are designed to be simple to most purely measure processing speed. Higher scores indicate faster speed of processing. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Pattern Comparison Processing Speed Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: DDisc_SV_1mo_200
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Self-regulation/Impulsivity (Delay Discounting) | columnHeader: DDisc_SV_1mo_200 | description: Delay Discounting: Subjective Value for $200 at 1 month. Participants choice of an immediate amount that is equivalent to receiving $200 at 1 month. In this task, participants are presented with two choices on each trial - a smaller amount today or $200 at 1 month. Using an adjusting-amount approach, delays are fixed and reward amounts are adjusted on a trial-by-trial basis based on participants choices, to rapidly hone in on an indifference point that indicates the Subjective immediate Value of $200 in 1 month. Delay discounting describes the undervaluing of rewards that are delayed in time. It is illustrated by the fact that humans (and other animals) will often choose a smaller immediate reward over an objectively larger, but delayed reward. | fullDisplayName: Delay Discounting: Subjective Value for $200 at 1 month (DDisc_SV_1mo_200)

Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Self-regulation/Impulsivity (Delay Discounting) | columnHeader: DDisc_SV_5yr_40K | description: Delay Discounting: Subjective Value for $40K at 5 years. Participants choice of an immediate amount that is equivalent to receiving $40000 in 5 years. In this task, participants are presented with two choices on each trial - a smaller amount today or $40K in 5 years. Using an adjusting-amount approach, delays are fixed and reward amounts are adjusted on a trial-by-trial basis based on participants choices, to rapidly hone in on an indifference point that indicates the Subjective immediate Value of $40K in 5 years. Delay discounting describes the undervaluing of rewards that are delayed in time. It is illustrated by the fact that humans (and other animals) will often choose a smaller immediate reward over an objectively larger, but delayed reward. | fullDisplayName: Delay Discounting: Subjective Value for $40K at 5 years (DDisc_SV_5yr_40K)

It is illustrated by the fact that humans (and other animals) will often choose a smaller immediate reward over an objectively larger, but delayed reward. | fullDisplayName: Delay Discounting: Subjective Value for $40K at 10 years (DDisc_SV_10yr_40K)

Delay Condition.name: ListSort_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Working Memory (List Sorting) | columnHeader: ListSort_Unadj | description: This task assesses working memory and requires the participant to sequence different visually- and orally-presented stimuli (foods and animals) into size order. List Sorting is a measure of working memory, tapping both information processing and storage. It is considered a “fluid ability” measure, with performance tending to peak in early adulthood and then declining across the life span. Higher scores on each of these indicate higher levels of working memory. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox List Sorting Working Memory Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: ListSort_AgeAdj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Working Memory (List Sorting) | columnHeader: ListSort_AgeAdj | description: This task assesses working memory and requires the participant to sequence different visually- and orally-presented stimuli (foods and animals) into size order. List Sorting is a measure of working memory, tapping both information processing and storage. It is considered a “fluid ability” measure, with performance tending to peak in early adulthood and then declining across the life span. Higher scores on each of these indicate higher levels of working memory. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox List Sorting Working Memory Test: Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: CogFluidComp_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Cognition Fluid Composite | columnHeader: CogFluidComp_Unadj | description: The Fluid Cognition Composite score is derived by averaging the normalized scores of each of the Toolbox tests that are fluid ability measures (Flanker, Dimensional Change Card Sort, Picture Sequence Memory, List Sorting and Pattern Comparison), then deriving scale scores based on this new distribution. One can interpret the Fluid Cognition Composite as a more global assessment of individual and group fluid cognition functioning. Higher scores indicate higher levels of functioning. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Cognition Fluid Composite: Unadjusted Scale Score

One can interpret the Fluid Cognition Composite as a more global assessment of individual and group fluid cognition functioning. Higher scores indicate higher levels of functioning. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Cognition Fluid Composite: Age Adjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: CogEarlyComp_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Cognition Early Childhood Composite | columnHeader: CogEarlyComp_Unadj | description: The Early Childhood Composite score is derived by averaging the normalized scores of each of the four Toolbox cognition measures that comprise the Early Childhood Battery (Picture Vocabulary, Flanker, DCCS and Picture Sequence Memory), then deriving scale scores based on this new distribution. The Early Childhood Composite Score can be interpreted much like the Cognitive Function Composite and it provides a highly reliable overall snapshot of general cognitive functioning. Higher scores indicate higher levels of functioning. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Cognition Early Childhood Composite: Unadjusted Scale Score

The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more loneliness. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of loneliness and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of loneliness. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Loneliness Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).Loneliness_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Delay Condition.name: PercHostil_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: PercHostil_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses perceptions of hostility in daily social interactions (e.g., how often people argue with me, yell at me, or criticize me) using an 8-item fixed-length form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of greater perceived hostility. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of perceived hostility and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of perceived hostility. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Perceived Hostility Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).PercHostil_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Delay Condition.name: PercReject_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: PercReject_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses perceptions of rejection in daily social interactions (e.g., how often people don’t listen when I ask for help, or do not pay attention to me) using an 8-item fixed-length form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of greater perceived rejection. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of perceived rejection and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of perceived rejection. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Perceived Rejection Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).PercReject_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Delay Condition.name: EmotSupp_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: EmotSupp_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses emotional support (refers to the perception that people in one’s social network are available to listen to one’s problems with empathy, caring and understanding) through an 8-item fixed-length form for ages 18-85. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more emotional support. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of support,and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of support. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Emotional Support Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).EmotSupp_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Delay Condition.name: InstruSupp_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development) | columnHeader: InstruSupp_Unadj | description: This self-report measure assesses instrumental support (refers to the perception that people in one’s social network are available to provide material or functional aid in completing daily tasks, if needed) for ages 18-85, using an 8-item fixed-length form. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. higher scores are indicative of more reported support. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of instrumental supportand scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of instrumental support. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Instrumental Support Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development).InstruSupp_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Social Relationships (Social Support, Companionship, Social Distress, Positive Social Development)

Delay Condition.name: PercStress_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy) | columnHeader: PercStress_Unadj | description: This is a self-report measure administered to ages 13-85 in CAT form that accesses how unpredictable, uncontrollable and overloadedrespondents find their lives. The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more perceived stress. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of perceived stress and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of perceived stress. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Perceived Stress Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy).PercStress_Unadj
metadata.group_path: Emotion > Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy)

Delay Condition.name: SelfEff_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Emotion | assessment: Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy) | columnHeader: SelfEff_Unadj | description: This is a self-report measure administered to ages 8-85 in CAT form. It assesses respondents' sense of global self-efficacy (a person’s belief in his/her capacity to manage functioning and have control over meaningful events). The Unadjusted Scale Score has a Mean of 50, SD of 10. Higher scores are indicative of more general self-efficacy. Scores 1 SD or more below the mean (T ≤ 40) suggest low levels of general self-efficacy and scores 1 SD or more above the mean (T ≥ 60) suggest high levels of general self-efficacy. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Self-Efficacy Survey: Unadjusted Scale Score

metadata.leaf_id: Emotion > Stress and Self Efficacy (Perceived Stress, Self-Efficacy).SelfEff_Unadj

Delay Condition.name: Endurance_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Endurance (2 minute walk test) | columnHeader: Endurance_Unadj | description: This test measures sub-maximal cardiovascular endurance by recording the distance that the participant is able to walk on a 50-foot (out and back) course in 2 minutes.  The participant's raw score is the distance in feet and inches walked in 2 minutes. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant raw score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 2-minute Walk Endurance Test : Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: Endurance_AgeAdj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Endurance (2 minute walk test) | columnHeader: Endurance_AgeAdj | description: This test measures sub-maximal cardiovascular endurance by recording the distance that the participant is able to walk on a 50-foot (out and back) course in 2 minutes.  The participant's raw score is the distance in feet and inches walked in 2 minutes. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant raw score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 2-minute Walk Endurance Test : Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: GaitSpeed_Comp
Delay Condition.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Locomotion (4-meter walk test) | columnHeader: GaitSpeed_Comp | description: Participants are asked to walk a short distance (4 meters) at their usual pace. Raw scores are recorded as the time in seconds required to walk 4 meters on each of the two trials, with the better trial used for scoring (raw scores are available on request to info@humanconnectome.org). Computed scores are in meters per second. On the 4-Meter Walk Gait Speed Test, higher computed scores are indicative of better gait speed (i.e., fewer seconds to walk four meters). | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 4-Meter Walk Gait Speed Test: Computed Score

Delay Condition.name: Dexterity_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Dexterity (9-hole Pegboard) | columnHeader: Dexterity_Unadj | description: This test of manual dexterity records the time required for the participant to accurately place and remove 9 plastic pegs into a plastic pegboard. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. Higher scoring is indicative of better dexterity in the dominant hand. For the 9-Hole Pegboard Dexterity Test, the raw score, recorded as time in seconds that it takes the participant to complete the task, is commonly used for interpretation, with faster completion times (less time to complete) representing better manual dexterity. This also allows for raw comparisons between dominant and non-dominant hand performance. Raw scores are available on request to info@humanconnectome.org. However, one can also evaluate performance with the dominant hand by looking at the normative scale scores provided here. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 9-hole Pegboard Dexterity Test : Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: Dexterity_AgeAdj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Dexterity (9-hole Pegboard) | columnHeader: Dexterity_AgeAdj | description: This test of manual dexterity records the time required for the participant to accurately place and remove 9 plastic pegs into a plastic pegboard. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. Higher scoring is indicative of better dexterity in the dominant hand. For the 9-Hole Pegboard Dexterity Test, the raw score, recorded as time in seconds that it takes the participant to complete the task, is commonly used for interpretation, with faster completion times (less time to complete) representing better manual dexterity. This also allows for raw comparisons between dominant and non-dominant hand performance. Raw scores are available on request to info@humanconnectome.org. However, one can also evaluate performance with the dominant hand by looking at the normative scale scores provided here. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox 9-hole Pegboard Dexterity Test : Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: Strength_Unadj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Strength (Grip Strength Dynamometry) | columnHeader: Strength_Unadj | description: Grip strength for each hand is measured with the participant seated in a chair with their feet touching the ground. With the elbow bent to 90 degrees and the arm against the trunk, wrist at neutral, participants squeeze the Jamar Plus Digital dynamometer as hard as they can for a count of three. The dynamometer provides a digital reading of force in pounds.  each hand. The Grip Strength Test provides a raw score for each hand (raw scores available on request to info@humanconnectome.org), with the scale scores representing the relative force the participant was able to generate using his/her dominant hand. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant raw score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Grip Strength Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Delay Condition.name: Strength_AgeAdj
Delay Condition.desc: category: Motor | assessment: Strength (Grip Strength Dynamometry) | columnHeader: Strength_AgeAdj | description: Grip strength for each hand is measured with the participant seated in a chair with their feet touching the ground. With the elbow bent to 90 degrees and the arm against the trunk, wrist at neutral, participants squeeze the Jamar Plus Digital dynamometer as hard as they can for a count of three. The dynamometer provides a digital reading of force in pounds.  each hand. The Grip Strength Test provides a raw score for each hand (raw scores available on request to info@humanconnectome.org), with the scale scores representing the relative force the participant was able to generate using his/her dominant hand. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant raw score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Grip Strength Test: Age-Adjusted-Adjusted Scale Score

Note: 'Release' does not indicate the version of the pipelines used to process subject data. Check the release-notes distributed with each processed dataset for pipeline version information. | fullDisplayName: Quarter Released

Outcome Type.name: Acquisition
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Acquisition | description: Quarter in which this subject's 3T and behavioral data (and MEG, if applicable) was initially acquired. Quarter is defined as relative to the start of HCP Phase II data collection. Q1 = 8/2012 - 10/2012 (Aug-Oct), Q2 = 11/2012 - 1/2013, Q3 = 2/2013 - 4/2013, Q4 = 5/2013 - 7/2013, Q5 = 8/2013 - 10/2013, etc. | fullDisplayName: Acquisition Quarter

Outcome Type.name: Gender
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Gender | description: Gender of Subject | fullDisplayName: Gender

Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Age | description: Age group of Participant, banded in five-year increments. | fullDisplayName: Age Range

Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Age | description: Age group of Participant. | fullDisplayName: Age Range

Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Age | description: Age group of Participant. | fullDisplayName: Age Range

Outcome Type.name: Age_in_Yrs
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Age_in_Yrs | description: Age of Participant in Years | fullDisplayName: Age in Years

Outcome Type.name: HasGT
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: HasGT | description: Genotyping data available from at least one of the 2 gene chips used: (1) Illumina Mega Chip plus content from the Illumina ImmunoArray and PsychArray; (2) Illumina Neuro Consortium Array.   Family structure (i.e., Mother_ID and Father_ID) is genetically verified for pairs of siblings (includings twins and half-silbings) for whom HasGT=TRUE | fullDisplayName: HasGT

Outcome Type.name: ZygositySR
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: ZygositySR | description: Self-reported zygosity. Until the S1200 release this was the \Zygosity\" measure. The small number of subjects who do not have a value for this measure (blank) self reported as twins but did not self report their twin zygosity." | fullDisplayName: ZygositySR

Outcome Type.name: ZygosityGT
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: ZygosityGT | description: Twin zygosity verified by genotyping. Requires both subjects in a twin pair to have HasGT=TRUE to have a value (if genotyping is not available for either of a twin pair, no values are given for ZygosityGT).  Non-twin subjects also do not have a value for ZygosityGT.  Note that some subjects self-reported as dizygotic twins (ZygositySR='NotMZ') but genotyping established that they were monozygotic twins (thence ZygosityGT='MZ' for those subjects).  ZygosityGT should be given precedence over ZygositySR. | fullDisplayName: ZygosityGT

Outcome Type.name: Family_ID
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Family_ID | description: Lists all Mother_IDs and Father_IDs associated with subjects who share at least one parent. Family_ID does not indicate that siblings grew up in the same household/rearing environment. | fullDisplayName: FamilyID

Outcome Type.name: Mother_ID
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Mother_ID | description: Mother's study ID, based on genotyping between sibling subjects that both have HasGT=TRUE, otherwise is based on self report. Prior to the S1200 release, Mother_ID was based strictly on self-report.  Mother_IDs that have been changed in the S1200 release based on the genotyping released have a 999xx ID number | fullDisplayName: Mother ID

Outcome Type.name: Father_ID
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Father_ID | description: Father's study ID, based on genotyping between sibling subjects that both have HasGT=TRUE, otherwise is based on self report. Prior to the S1200 release, Father_ID was based strictly on self-report.  Father_IDs that have been changed in the S1200 release based on the genotyping released have a 999xx ID number | fullDisplayName: Father ID

Outcome Type.name: TestRetestInterval
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: TestRetestInterval | description: Time interval in days between the first scan session of subject's study \Test\" visit in the main HCP project and the first scan session of their \"Retest\" visit." | fullDisplayName: TestRetestInterval

Outcome Type.name: Handedness
Outcome Type.desc: category: Subject Information | assessment: Demographics | columnHeader: Handedness | description: Handedness of participant from -100 to 100 assessed using the Edinburgh Handedness questionnaire [Oldfield RC (1971). The assessment and analysis of handedness: The Edinburgh inventory. Neuropsychologia, 9(1): 97-113.]. Negative numbers indicate that a subject is more left-handed than right-handed, while positive numbers indicate that a subject is more right-handed than left-handed. See Schachter et al., Associations of handedness with hair color and learning disabilities. Neuropsychologia, 25:269-276, 1987. for more information on how these laterality scores are calculated. | fullDisplayName: Handedness

Outcome Type.desc: category: Study Completion | assessment: Task Version Info: 7T MR | columnHeader: 7T_fMRI_Mov_Vrs | description: Version of Movie stimuli displayed to subject. 1 is earlier version, 2 is improved version implemented mid-collection. In the first version (1) , the validation clip at the end of each run was not synched to be at a consistent time relative to the TR. In the second version (2), we shifted the final 80 seconds of each 15 minute movie by 0-200ms, depending on the movie to ensure the validation clip at the end of each run started at a consistent time relative to the TR and that all the movies end exactly on the TR (second). | fullDisplayName: 7T MR fMRI Movie Task Version

Outcome Type.name: QC_Issue
Outcome Type.desc: category: QC Issues | assessment: Quality Control Issue Noted | columnHeader: QC_Issue | description: Subjects with notable issues (coded A-E) found in the HCP Quality Control process. For more information, see the QC Issues wiki: A= Focal anatomical anomaly (e.g. calcification, cavernoma, cyst, etc.) found in T1w and/or T2w scans in a restricted brain region; B= Focal segmentation or surface error(s) in outputs from the HCP Structural pipeline; C= Subject data collected during period of known intermittent problems with head coil leading to temporal instability in acquisitions; D= One or more rfMRI scans contained significant coil- or movement-related artifact which manifests prominently in the \"minimally preprocessed\" data. FIX cleaned data has been confirmed to be reasonable for all these scans; E= Manual reclassification of some of the FIX-ICA components from 'signal' to 'noise' was necessary to appropriately remove artifact from the FIX-denoised rfMRI scans. | fullDisplayName: Coded QC Issue

Outcome Type.name: PicSeq_Unadj
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Episodic Memory (Picture Sequence Memory) | columnHeader: PicSeq_Unadj | description: The Picture Sequence Memory Test is an assessment of episodic memory for ages 3-85 years which involves the acquisition, storage and effortful recall of new information. It is considered a strong 'fluid ability' measure, with performance reaching a peak in early adulthood and declining across the life span. It involves recalling increasingly lengthy series of illustrated objects and activities that are presented in a particular order on the computer screen. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Picture Sequence Memory Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Outcome Type.name: PicSeq_AgeAdj
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Episodic Memory (Picture Sequence Memory) | columnHeader: PicSeq_AgeAdj | description: The Picture Sequence Memory Test is an assessment of episodic memory for ages 3-85 years which involves the acquisition, storage and effortful recall of new information. It is considered a strong 'fluid ability' measure, with performance reaching a peak in early adulthood and declining across the life span. It involves recalling increasingly lengthy series of illustrated objects and activities that are presented in a particular order on the computer screen. Age-Adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Picture Sequence Memory Test: Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Outcome Type.name: CardSort_Unadj
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Executive Function/Cognitive Flexibility (Dimensional Change Card Sort) | columnHeader: CardSort_Unadj | description: The Dimensional Change Card Sort Test is a measure of executive function, specifically tapping cognitive flexibility for ages 3-85. It is considered a 'fluid ability' measure, with performance generally increasing through childhood and then declining across the adult age span. Target pictures are presented that vary along two dimensions (e.g., shape and color). Participants are asked to match a series of bivalent test pictures (e.g., yellow balls and blue trucks) to the target pictures, first according to one dimension (e.g., color) and then, after a number of trials, according to the other dimension (e.g., shape). Switch trials are also employed, in which the participant must change the dimension being matched, thus requiring the cognitive flexibility to quickly choose the correct stimulus. Scoring is based on a combination of accuracy and reaction time. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Dimensional Change Card Sort Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Outcome Type.name: Flanker_Unadj
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Executive Function/Inhibition (Flanker Task) | columnHeader: Flanker_Unadj | description: The Flanker is a measure of executive function, specifically tapping inhibitory control and attention for ages 3-85. It is considered a fluid ability measure (i.e. measures the capacity for new learning and information processing in novel situations) in which performance reaches a peak in early adulthood, then tends to decline across the life span. The test requires the participant to focus on a given stimulus while inhibiting attention to stimuli (arrows for ages 8-85) flanking it. Sometimes the middle stimulus is pointing in the same direction as the flankers (congruent) and sometimes in the opposite direction (incongruent). Scoring is based on a combination of accuracy and reaction time. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Flanker Inhibitory Control and Attention Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Outcome Type.name: PMAT24_A_CR
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Fluid Intelligence (Penn Progressive Matrices) | columnHeader: PMAT24_A_CR | description: Penn Matrix Test (PMAT): Number of Correct Responses. The PMAT measures fluid intelligence via non-verbal reasoning using an abbreviated version of the Raven's Progressive Matrices Form A developed by Gur and colleagues (Bilker et al. 2012).  Participants are presented with patterns made up of 2x2, 3x3 or 1x5 arrangements of squares, with one of the squares missing.  The participant must pick one of five response choices that best fits the missing square on the pattern.  The task has 24 items and 3 bonus items, arranged in order of increasing difficulty.  However, the task discontinues if the participant makes 5 incorrect responses in a row. | fullDisplayName: Penn Progressive Matrices: Number of Correct Responses (PMAT24_A_CR)

Outcome Type.name: ReadEng_Unadj
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Language/Reading Decoding (Oral Reading Recognition) | columnHeader: ReadEng_Unadj | description: The Reading Test is a CAT format measure of reading decoding skill and of crystallized abilities, those abilities that are generally more dependent upon past learning experiences and consistent across the life span for ages 7-85. The participant is asked to read and pronounce letters and words as accurately as possible. Higher scores indicate better reading ability. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Oral Reading Recognition Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Language/Reading Decoding (Oral Reading Recognition) | columnHeader: ReadEng_AgeAdj | description: The Reading Test is a CAT format measure of reading decoding skill and of crystallized abilities, those abilities that are generally more dependent upon past learning experiences and consistent across the life span for ages 7-85. The participant is asked to read and pronounce letters and words as accurately as possible. Higher scores indicate better reading ability. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Oral Reading Recognition Test: Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Outcome Type.name: PicVocab_Unadj
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Language/Vocabulary Comprehension (Picture Vocabulary) | columnHeader: PicVocab_Unadj | description: The Picture Vocabulary Test is a CAT format measure of general vocabulary knowledge for ages 3-85 and is considered to be a strong measure of crystallized abilities (those abilities that are more dependent upon past learning experiences and are consistent across the lifespan). The participant is presented with an audio recording of a word and four photographic images on the computer screen and is asked to select the picture that most closely matches the meaning of the word. Higher scores indicate higher vocabulary ability. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Picture Vocabulary Test: Unadjusted Scale Score

Outcome Type.name: PicVocab_AgeAdj
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Language/Vocabulary Comprehension (Picture Vocabulary) | columnHeader: PicVocab_AgeAdj | description: The Picture Vocabulary Test is a CAT format measure of general vocabulary knowledge for ages 3-85 and is considered to be a strong measure of crystallized abilities (those abilities that are more dependent upon past learning experiences and are consistent across the lifespan). The participant is presented with an audio recording of a word and four photographic images on the computer screen and is asked to select the picture that most closely matches the meaning of the word. Higher scores indicate higher vocabulary ability. Age-adjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed using the age appropriate band of Toolbox Norming Sample (bands of ages 18-29, or 30-35), where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average for participants age band. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Picture Vocabulary Test: Age-Adjusted Scale Score

Outcome Type.name: ProcSpeed_Unadj
Outcome Type.desc: category: Cognition | assessment: Processing Speed (Pattern Completion Processing Speed) | columnHeader: ProcSpeed_Unadj | description: The Pattern Comparison Processing Test is a measure of speed of processing, which is considered a 'fluid ability' because it steadily improves (time to complete task decreases) throughout childhood and adolescence, then begins to decline in adulthood. This test for ages 7-85 asks participants to discern whether two side-by-side pictures are the same or not. The items are designed to be simple to most purely measure processing speed. Higher scores indicate faster speed of processing. Unadjusted Scale Score: Participant score is normed to those in the entire NIH Toolbox Normative Sample (18 and older), regardless of age or any other variable, where a score of 100 indicates performance that was at the national average and a score of 115 or 85, indicates performance 1 SD above or below the national average. | fullDisplayName: NIH Toolbox Pattern Comparison Processing Speed Test: Unadjusted Scale Score
